article.title: CISA Adds cPanel Authentication Bypass to KEV as Sorry Ransomware Crew and a Suspected Espionage Actor Hit 44,000 Servers
article.summary: CVE-2026-41940, a 9.8-rated authentication bypass exploited as a zero-day since late February, is now driving Sorry ransomware infections and a separate espionage campaign against South-East Asian government targets.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A critical authentication bypass in cPanel and WebHost Manager (WHM), tracked as CVE-2026-41940, has moved from quiet zero-day abuse into open mass exploitation. cPanel released emergency patches on April 28, 2026, according to [CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/cpanel-authentication-bypass-vulnerability-cve-2026-41940-exploited/). Two days later, on April 30, 2026, the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency added the bug to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with a federal-agency patch deadline of May 3, 2026, [as recorded in the NVD entry](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-41940). By that point, more than 40,000 servers had likely already been compromised, [SecurityWeek reported](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/).

## What We Know

### The vulnerability

The National Vulnerability Database describes CVE-2026-41940 as an authentication bypass in the login flow that lets unauthenticated remote attackers gain access to the cPanel control panel, [per NIST](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-41940). NIST records a CVSS 3.1 base score of 9.8 and a CVSS 4.0 base score of 9.3, both rated Critical, and classifies the weakness as CWE-306, Missing Authentication for Critical Function, with VulnCheck listed as the CVE assigner and a publication date of April 29, 2026, [also from the NVD record](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-41940).

[CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/cpanel-authentication-bypass-vulnerability-cve-2026-41940-exploited/) describes the underlying flaw as stemming from improper handling of user input during the login process: when a user attempts to log in, cPanel writes data from the request into a server-side session file before verifying the user's identity. [SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/) summarises the resulting technique as the use of special characters in authorization headers to write parameters to a session file, then triggering a reload of that file.

### Affected and patched versions

All cPanel and WHM releases after version 11.40, plus WP Squared, are affected, [according to CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/cpanel-authentication-bypass-vulnerability-cve-2026-41940-exploited/). [SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/) lists the patched cPanel and WHM builds as 11.110.0.97, 11.118.0.63, 11.126.0.54, 11.132.0.29, 11.134.0.20 and 11.136.0.5, with WP Squared addressed in 136.1.7. Rapid7 estimated roughly 1.5 million cPanel instances accessible from the internet, [also via SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/).

### Zero-day window

The bug was a true zero-day for roughly two months. Hosting provider KnownHost said successful exploits had been observed in the wild prior to any fix being made available, [as relayed by CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/cpanel-authentication-bypass-vulnerability-cve-2026-41940-exploited/), and both [SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/) and [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/critrical-cpanel-flaw-mass-exploited-in-sorry-ransomware-attacks/) trace observed exploitation back to late February. Activity escalated sharply once the advisory and a watchTowr technical write-up were public, [SecurityWeek noted](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/).

### Scale of compromise

The Shadowserver Foundation reported at least 44,000 IP addresses running cPanel had since been compromised, [BleepingComputer wrote](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/critrical-cpanel-flaw-mass-exploited-in-sorry-ransomware-attacks/). [SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/) put the figure at more than 40,000 servers and added that most affected systems are in the U.S., with France and the Netherlands rounding out the top three.

### Sorry ransomware

[BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/critrical-cpanel-flaw-mass-exploited-in-sorry-ransomware-attacks/) describes one of the post-exploitation payloads as a Go-based Linux encryptor known as Sorry ransomware that appends a .sorry extension to encrypted files. The same outlet reports the malware uses a ChaCha20 stream cipher with RSA-2048 public-key protection, and quotes ransomware researcher Rivitna saying that decryption is impossible without an RSA-2048 private key. Victims are instructed to make contact via Tox, [BleepingComputer adds](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/critrical-cpanel-flaw-mass-exploited-in-sorry-ransomware-attacks/).

### Suspected espionage activity

A separate, more targeted campaign emerged in parallel. Threat-intelligence firm Ctrl-Alt-Intel detected on May 2, 2026 an unknown threat actor interactively targeting government and military entities in South-East Asia along with managed service providers and hosting providers, [The Hacker News reported](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/critical-cpanel-vulnerability.html). Targets included Philippine military domains under *.mil.ph and Laotian government domains under *.gov.la, with additional MSP victims spanning the Philippines, Laos, Canada, South Africa and the United States, [also via The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/critical-cpanel-vulnerability.html). Activity originated from the IP address 95.111.250[.]175, used publicly available proof-of-concept code, and deployed the AdaptixC2 framework along with persistence tooling such as OpenVPN and Ligolo, [The Hacker News wrote](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/critical-cpanel-vulnerability.html). Around 110 files totalling 4.37GB were stolen in one observed intrusion, [the same report says](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/critical-cpanel-vulnerability.html).

## What We Don't Know

Neither [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/critical-cpanel-vulnerability.html) nor [CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/cpanel-authentication-bypass-vulnerability-cve-2026-41940-exploited/) attributes the South-East Asia espionage activity to a named country or threat group. The overlap, if any, between operators of the Sorry ransomware crew and the espionage cluster is also not specified by the cited sources. Compromise counts from [Shadowserver](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/critrical-cpanel-flaw-mass-exploited-in-sorry-ransomware-attacks/) reflect IP addresses observed scanning or exhibiting compromise indicators against honeypots and visible internet hosts; the true number of cPanel servers fully owned by attackers may be higher or lower depending on detection coverage.

## Why It Matters

cPanel and WHM remain the dominant Linux hosting control plane for shared and reseller environments, with [Rapid7's roughly 1.5 million internet-exposed instances figure](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/) underlining the blast radius. A pre-authentication, network-reachable bypass scoring 9.8 on CVSS 3.1, [as catalogued by NIST](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-41940), in software with that footprint guarantees both opportunistic ransomware operators and at least one apparently directed actor will keep running scans long after the [CISA-mandated May 3 federal deadline](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-41940). Hosting providers and customers running self-managed cPanel installations need to confirm they are on one of the patched builds enumerated by [SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/over-40000-servers-compromised-in-ongoing-cpanel-exploitation/) and assume any unpatched server reachable since late February has been touched.
